title: Cisco IOS Interface Configuration
description: Learn how to configure switch and router interface properties including descriptions, speed, duplex, VLAN assignment, administrative state, bandwidth, MTU, and event logging.

tasks.hint: Use the command: 'description Uplink-to-Core' or 'desc Uplink-to-Core'
tasks.explanation: The 'description' command adds a human-readable label to an interface. It appears in 'show interfaces' output and helps document network topology.

tasks.prompt: Configure the interface to operate in full-duplex mode.
tasks.explanation: Full-duplex allows simultaneous send and receive on the link. A duplex mismatch with the connected device causes performance problems and CRC errors.

tasks.prompt: Set the interface speed to 100 Mbps.
tasks.explanation: The 'speed' command hard-sets the port rate. On auto-negotiating links, manually fixing speed (and duplex) prevents mismatches with legacy devices.

tasks.prompt: Configure this port as an access port (carries traffic for a single VLAN).
tasks.hint: Use the command: 'switchport mode access' or 'sw mod acc'
tasks.explanation: Access ports connect end devices and carry untagged traffic for one VLAN. This is the default role for user-facing switch ports.

tasks.prompt: Assign this access port to VLAN 20.
tasks.hint: Use the command: 'switchport access vlan 20' or 'sw acc vlan 20'
tasks.explanation: The 'switchport access vlan' command places an access port into the specified VLAN, isolating its traffic from other broadcast domains.
The 'shutdown' command places the interface in an administratively down state. The link LED goes off and no traffic passes until 'no shutdown' is applied.

tasks.prompt: Enable (bring up) this administratively disabled interface.
tasks.hint: Use the command: 'no shutdown' or 'no shut'
tasks.explanation: Newly created or previously shut down interfaces require 'no shutdown' to transition from administratively down to up, allowing the physical link to operate.

tasks.prompt: Set the interface bandwidth metric to 10000 kilobits per second (used by routing protocols).
tasks.explanation: The 'bandwidth' command sets a logical metric for EIGRP and OSPF cost calculations—it does not change the actual physical port speed.

tasks.prompt: Set the maximum transmission unit (MTU) to 1500 bytes on this interface.
tasks.explanation: MTU defines the largest Layer 3 packet the interface can carry. The default Ethernet MTU of 1500 bytes is standard for most IPv4 networks.

tasks.prompt: Enable syslog messages when this interface's link status changes.
tasks.hint: Use the command: 'logging events link-status' or 'log events link-status'
tasks.explanation: The 'logging events link-status' command generates a syslog entry whenever the interface transitions between up and down, aiding link-flap diagnosis.
